concept.title: Postman Interface
concept.shortdesc: The Postman interface consists of a sidebar, a request builder, and a response viewer. Understanding the layout helps you navigate collections and build requests efficiently.

section.title: Main Interface Areas
section.p: The Postman desktop application is divided into the following areas:

dlentry.dd: The left panel contains your Collections, Environments, APIs, Mock Servers, and Monitors. Use the sidebar to navigate between saved requests and folders.
The central area is the workbench, where you open and edit requests, write test scripts, and view responses. Requests open as tabs across the top of the workbench.
Within the workbench, the request builder contains the URL bar, HTTP method selector, and tabs for Params, Authorization, Headers, Body, Pre-request Script, and Tests.
Below the request builder, the response viewer displays the response body, status code, response time, and size. Tabs include Body, Cookies, Headers, and Test Results.
Located in the upper-right corner, the environment selector lets you switch the active environment. The current environment name appears in the dropdown.
The bottom bar shows connection status, Postman version, and access to the Console, which logs request and response details for debugging.

section.title: Request Builder Tabs
section.p: Each request in Postman has the following configuration tabs:

strow.stentry: Add query parameters as key-value pairs. Postman appends them to the URL automatically.
Configure authentication, including API keys, Bearer tokens, Basic Auth, and OAuth 2.0.
Add or override HTTP request headers.
Specify the request body for POST, PUT, and PATCH requests. Supports raw JSON, form-data, and binary.
Write JavaScript that runs before the request is sent, such as setting variables or generating tokens.
Write JavaScript assertions that run after the response is received to validate the API behavior.
